quote: Hey, Glenn. How are you doing?
stewart_read: The rep opens with the wrong name. The homeowner on file is Lee Yoon; the person who answered is Randy. This is not a minor slip — it is the root cause of the customer's adversarial posture for the entire call. Both intro templates in the schema require a correct name as the first legitimacy signal. The rep failed that before the first sentence was complete.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1 and ask the rep: 'Where did Glenn come from?' The rep needs a pre-call habit — confirm the name on the lead record before dialing, and if the person who answers gives a different name, acknowledge it immediately ('Sorry about that, Randy — I had a different name on my end. Let me make sure I have the right person'). This is a 10-second fix that changes the entire opening dynamic.

quote: But what is your job? Just to get appointment?
stewart_read: The customer has correctly diagnosed the rep's role and is now testing whether the rep will own it or deflect. The rep confirmed honestly ('Yeah. Yeah.') but missed the reframe opportunity entirely. The setter scope is not a weakness to apologize for — it is a customer-benefit structure ('the specialist knows more than I do, so you get the right person for the right conversation'). The rep's flat confirmation left the customer with 'I'm talking to a gatekeeper' rather than 'this process is designed to serve me.'
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep confirmed scope honestly to avoid over-promising. Rejected — there was no follow-up reframe, so the honest answer landed as a concession rather than a positioning move.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep on the universal escape hatch from setter_scope_discipline: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — I'm scheduling, they're solving.' The rep needs a one-sentence bridge that converts scope acknowledgment into a reason to stay on the call.

quote: We have them all cut. We only got five trees. Yeah. Yeah.
stewart_read: The customer says trees have been cut — but the rep's follow-up ('And it's still, like, really tall ones?') and the customer's response ('Yeah. There is. It's like it looks like yeah. We we only cut all the trees now') indicate ongoing ambiguity about shading. The customer is describing a partial tree removal, not a cleared site. This is not a confirmed candidacy win — it is an unresolved shading question. The rep moved on without clarifying whether the remaining five trees create meaningful shading on the roof plane.
alternative_hypothesis: Enthusiasm signal read: trees removed = improved solar candidacy. Rejected — the follow-up exchange confirms tall trees remain and shading status is unresolved.
coaching_implication: The rep should have asked one clarifying question: 'Are those remaining trees on the south or west side of the house?' That determines whether shading is a real issue. Moving on without that answer leaves the closer walking into a potential site-qualification problem. Flag for the closer to verify shading on site visit.

stewart_read: New roof is a strong positive qualifier — no re-roof required, no timing complication, solar-ready surface. The rep acknowledged it ('Okay. Awesome.') and moved on. This was the first genuinely positive signal in the call and the rep let it pass without anchoring it as a reason the customer is a strong candidate. A brief acknowledgment ('That actually makes the install cleaner — no roof work needed first') would have built momentum at a moment the call needed it.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep to use positive qualifier moments as mini-momentum builders. The customer volunteered this — it cost nothing and the rep walked past it. A single sentence anchoring the new roof as a candidacy advantage would have shifted the customer's posture at a low-cost moment.

quote: So we get everything we need off of your recent bill with Dominion.
stewart_read: The customer stated their utility as 'Millennium' at 04:25. The rep then refers to it as 'Dominion' at 05:07 — a different utility entirely. On a customer who is already skeptical of the rep's competence, getting the utility name wrong after the customer just said it is a material accuracy failure. The rep either wasn't listening or defaulted to a regional assumption. Either way, the customer now has a second data point (after the wrong name) that the rep doesn't know who they're dealing with.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1: play 04:25 and 05:07 back to back. Ask the rep what utility the customer said. This is an active listening gap, not a knowledge gap. The fix is simple — repeat the utility name back when the customer gives it ('Got it, Millennium') to confirm it landed. Also flag for the closer: the utility on file may be wrong; verify before the proposal.

quote: Oh, man. You're gonna be a salesperson. You gotta do better than that, man.
stewart_read: The customer is explicitly coaching the rep in real time. The rep's fumble — 'So we do have a set schedule. Let me quickly get this pulled up. It'll just be a quick second here' — broke the customer's patience. The rep's response ('Sorry about that') was minimal and moved immediately to the time slot without any acknowledgment of the customer's frustration. No rapport repair attempt was made. The appointment was reached despite this, not because of how the rep handled it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep absorbed the criticism and moved efficiently to close the booking rather than dwelling on the repair. Partially supported — the rep did get to the time slot quickly. But the absence of any acknowledgment of the customer's frustration means the closer inherits a customer who ended the call on a critical note.
coaching_implication: Coach the rep on two things: (1) know the schedule before the call — fumbling availability live is avoidable preparation failure; (2) when a customer calls out your performance, a one-sentence acknowledgment ('Fair point — let me have that ready') costs nothing and prevents the customer from ending the call feeling like they had to manage the rep.

quote: And once I get that bill from you, I'll just shoot you a confirmation email back.
stewart_read: The appointment is left conditional on bill receipt. No button-up elements were completed: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no no-driving/at-computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise. The rep's close is 'I'll confirm once I have the bill' — which means the Saturday 9:30 slot is not actually booked, it is a placeholder pending a document the customer has not yet sent. The customer ended the call with 'Alright. I gotta go' at 05:56, signaling time pressure that the rep did not convert into a locked commitment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep accepted the conditional close because the customer was time-pressured and a full button-up would have lost the call entirely. Partially supported — the customer did signal urgency at 05:56. But the rep could have compressed the button-up to the two highest-impact elements (verbal handshake + no-driving anchor) in 20 seconds rather than skipping it entirely.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the button_up checklist and identify which two elements have the highest show-rate impact when time is short. The 'is that fair' handshake and the no-driving/at-computer anchor are the minimum viable close. The rep needs a compressed button-up for time-pressured customers — not a full skip.

stewart_read: The customer gives a vague bill figure ('around 3 something,' then 'more than 300') and the rep acknowledges with 'Okay' and moves directly to the prior-designs question. This is the universal bill-anchor failure pattern identified across the corpus: capture without flip. The customer's bill pain — $300+ monthly — is the emotional lever for the entire pitch, and the rep let it pass as a data point. No alignment-against-utility, no curiosity creation, no flip.
coaching_implication: This is the floor-wide coaching priority per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring. The rep captured the number and dropped it. Suggested reframe direction (no fabricated numbers): anchor the pain the number represents — 'So you're sending $300-plus every month to the utility and getting nothing back for it. That's exactly what this program is designed to replace.' The flip doesn't require knowing the exact number; it requires naming the ongoing cost as the real problem.
